coredata.dc:title: Ultrasound treatment of crystalline oil-in-water emulsions stabilized by sodium caseinate: Impact on emulsion stability through altered crystallization behavior in the oil globules
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Ying
Xiao, Jiawei
Li, Lin
Wan, Liting
Li, Bing
Zhang, Xia
coredata.dc:description: Partial coalescence is a key factor contributing to the instability of crystalline oil-in-water emulsions in products like dressings and sauces, reducing shelf life. The intrinsic characteristics of semi-crystalline droplets, including solid fat content, fat crystal arrangement, and polymorphism, play a pivotal role in influencing partial coalescence, challenging prevention efforts even with emulsifiers like amphiphilic proteins. High-intensity ultrasound (HIU) has emerged as an efficient and cost-effective technology for manipulating bulk fat crystallization, thereby enhancing physical properties. This study specifically investigates the impact of HIU treatment on fat crystallization on protein-stabilized crystalline emulsions, utilizing palm olein stearin (POSt) as the lipid phase and sodium caseinate (NaCas) as the surfactant under various HIU powers (100, 150, 200, 300, and 400 W). Results show that increasing HIU power maintained the interfacial potential (−20 mV) provided by NaCas in the emulsions without significant differences. Higher HIU power induced the most stable polymorphic form (β) in the emulsions. Engagingly, the emulsions at 200 W exhibited better storage stability and slower partial coalescence kinetics. Semi-crystalline globules had more uniform and integral crystal clusters that were distributed tangentially near the droplet boundary, perhaps attributed to intermediate subcooling (40.4 °C) at 200 W. The acoustic energy of HIU significantly translates into thermal effects, influencing subcooling degrees as a dominant factor affecting crystallisation in the emulsions. This study establishes ultrasonic crystallization as a novel strategy for modifying the stability of emulsions containing fat crystals.
coredata.dcterms:subject: High-intensity ultrasound
Palm olein stearin
Oil-in-water emulsions
Subcooling degrees
Ultrasonic crystallization

Storage stability of POSt-based emulsions treated with HIU
Partially-coalesced kinetic of POSt-based emulsions treated with HIU
Interface potential of POSt-based emulsions treated with HIU
Crystal microstructure of POSt-based emulsions treated with HIU
Crystal polymorphism of POSt-based emulsions treated with HIU
Thermal behavior of POSt-based emulsions treated with HIU
Crystal formation of POSt-based emulsions treated with HIU
Emulsification mechanisms for HIU induced POSt-based emulsions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photograph of oil–water mixtures stored for 1 day (upper) and 180 days (bottom) at 4 °C (A). Mean particle size (B) of E150, E200,E300, and E400 at different storage times. Different capital letters indicate significant differences (p < 0.05) from different ultrasound power on the same day. Different lowercase letters indicate significant differences (p < 0.05) from the same power on different days. For abbreviations, refer to Table 1.
Particle size distribution of E150, E200,E300, and E400 at different storage times: 1d (A), 7d (B), 14 d (C), 28 d (D), 42 d (E), 180 d (F). For abbreviations, see Table 1.
ζ-potential of NaCas and POSt-based emulsions with different ultrasonic power stored for 1 day at 4 °C. For abbreviations, see Table 1.

After 24 h crystallization, microstructure of E150, E200, E300, and E400 under ordinary light field (the left image) and corresponding polarized light (the right image). Scale bar = 20 μm. For abbreviations, see Table 1.
Diffractogram for short spacings of bulk POSt and POSt-based emulsions by XRD after crystallization at 4 °C. For abbreviations, see Table 1.
The DSC heating curves of bulk POSt and POSt-based emulsions with different power, depicted at 5 °C/min rate. For abbreviations, see Table 1.
Possible mechanisms for HIU impacts on POSt-based emulsions stability by altering crystallization behavior.
Formulative and manufacturing parameters of all prepared samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Contral, bulk POSt; E100, E150, E200, E300, and E400, represents HIU-treated emulsions as the ultrasonic power increased from 0 to 400 W.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Melting parameters for bulk POSt and POSt-based emulsions with different power.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: For abbreviations, see Table 1. Tp is the melting peak temperature. To calculate enthalpy of heat flow (ΔH) between onset temperature (Ton) and offset temperature (Toff) using TA Instruments universal analytical software.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Temperatures of POSt-based emulsions treated for 5 min at different ultrasonic powers.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: For abbreviations, see Table 1. The values show mean ± standard deviation (n = 3). Means with different letters within the same column are statistically different (p < 0.05).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SFC of POSt-based emulsions with different ultrasonic power stored at 4 °C for 24 h.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: For abbreviations, see Table 1. The values show mean ± standard deviation (n = 3). Means with different letters within the same column are statistically different (p < 0.05).

head.ce:title: Ultrasound treatment of crystalline oil-in-water emulsions stabilized by sodium caseinate: Impact on emulsion stability through altered crystallization behavior in the oil globules

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food Science and Engineering, Guangdong Province Key Laboratory for Green Processing of Natural Products and Product Safety, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510640, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Engineering
Guangdong Province Key Laboratory for Green Processing of Natural Products and Product Safety

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food Science and Engineering, Guangdong Province Key Laboratory for Green Processing of Natural Products and Product Safety, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510640, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering and Energy Technology, Dongguan University of Technology, College Road 1, Dongguan 523808, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Energy Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering and Energy Technology, Dongguan University of Technology, College Road 1, Dongguan 523808, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Partial coalescence is a key factor contributing to the instability of crystalline oil-in-water emulsions in products like dressings and sauces, reducing shelf life. The intrinsic characteristics of semi-crystalline droplets, including solid fat content, fat crystal arrangement, and polymorphism, play a pivotal role in influencing partial coalescence, challenging prevention efforts even with emulsifiers like amphiphilic proteins. High-intensity ultrasound (HIU) has emerged as an efficient and cost-effective technology for manipulating bulk fat crystallization, thereby enhancing physical properties. This study specifically investigates the impact of HIU treatment on fat crystallization on protein-stabilized crystalline emulsions, utilizing palm olein stearin (POSt) as the lipid phase and sodium caseinate (NaCas) as the surfactant under various HIU powers (100, 150, 200, 300, and 400 W). Results show that increasing HIU power maintained the interfacial potential (−20 mV) provided by NaCas in the emulsions without significant differences. Higher HIU power induced the most stable polymorphic form (β) in the emulsions. Engagingly, the emulsions at 200 W exhibited better storage stability and slower partial coalescence kinetics. Semi-crystalline globules had more uniform and integral crystal clusters that were distributed tangentially near the droplet boundary, perhaps attributed to intermediate subcooling (40.4 °C) at 200 W. The acoustic energy of HIU significantly translates into thermal effects, influencing subcooling degrees as a dominant factor affecting crystallisation in the emulsions. This study establishes ultrasonic crystallization as a novel strategy for modifying the stability of emulsions containing fat crystals.

ce:section.ce:para: The traditional emulsions, containing of two-phase liquids, are recognized as thermodynamically unstable colloidal systems [1]. The food industry employs various emulsion types, such as oil-in-water (O/W), water-in-oil (W/O), water-in-oil-in-water (W/O/W), and oil-in-water-in-oil (O/W/O) [2]. In significant emulsions, part of the oil may crystallize at specific temperatures due to triglycerides reach a supercooled state [3]. These emulsions can transformed into three-phase systems, termed “solid fat-based emulsions”, where the presence of crystals in oil globules can substantially exacerbate the instability of the O/W emulsion [4]. The most plausible mechanism involves crystals protruding from the O/W interface, piercing the interface film between oil globules [5]. The crystalline structure resists capillary forces, preventing complete coalescence and leading to partial coalescence [6,7]. The arrangement of fat crystal in semi-crystalline globules, which influences susceptibility to partial coalescence, are classified into four main types based on polarised microscope: (1) in the O-type no crystals are present or they are so tiny to be seen, (2) in the N-type most crystals are actually platelet-like, (3) in the L-type crystals are tangentially oriented near the globule surface and (4) the M−type crystals have both L-type and N-type characteristics [8]. Crystallization behaviors of fat globules, including fat crystal shape, size and content, are crucial factors conditioning partial coalescence occurrence, and governing emulsion stability [9].

High-intensity ultrasound (HIU) serves as a supplementary technique for fat crystallization, drawing increased attention in the food industry [10]. With low frequency (20–100 kHz) and high power (10–10000 W/cm2), HIU offers benefits such as high energy efficiency, ease of operation, and cleaning [11,12]. HIU's cavitation process, including bubble generation, growth, and collapse, is considered the main factor affecting fat crystallization [13]. Studies on anhydrous milk fat and palm oil crystallization have shown that HIU not only reduces the induction time of crystallization but also favors the formation of small crystals [14,15]. HIU treatments induce stable crystal forms and increase the melting enthalpy of the anhydrous milk fat samples [16]. Palm olein stearin (POSt), widely used in the food industry for its wide availability, low cost, natural safety [17], was selected as the lipid phase. POSt contains high melting point triglyceride mixtures, resulting in diverse crystal structures due to molecular composition differences. This diversity forms the foundation for HIU to alter the polymorphic behavior of edible lipids [18]. These results indicate that ultrasound treatments positively adjust the crystallization behavior of bulk fats. However, few studies have explored the influence of HIU treatments on fat crystallization in emulsified states, particularly whether the stabilization of crystalline emulsion is affected by the interface behaviors of fat crystals.

Sodium caseinate (NaCas), consisting of αs1- and αs2-caseins, β-casein, and κ-casein, is a well-used nutritional and functional emulsifier in crystalline emulsion with relatively high solubility and fast adsorption at the oil–water interface [19]. Milk fat emulsions stabilized with NaCas have been reported to maintain the same thermal properties as the bulk milk fat, with a balance between liquid and crystallized fat, which could be used to enhance the sensory quality of fatty foods [20]. It has been widely proven that HIU could physically alter the interfacial properties of NaCas, enhancing the amount of adsorbed protein, thus contributing to the improvement of emulsion stability [21]. However, recent studies found that HIU also showed the effect of ultrasonic crystallization on the emulsion stability [22,23], probably related to modifying partial coalescence of crystalline droplets. Thus, the stabilization mechanism of crystalline oil-in-water with NaCas formed by HIU needs further study. In this study, POSt and NaCas were chosen as the lipid phase and the amphiphilic surfactant, respectively, to prepare O/W emulsions using the HIU treatment. The primary objective is to evaluate the effect of HIU on fat crystallization in POSt-based emulsions, focusing on crystal morphology, polymorphism, arrangement and thermal properties. The study aims to predict the behavioral correlation between crystallization and emulsification for emulsion stabilization.

POSt with a melting point of 51.0 ± 0.2 °C was obtained from Lean Oil Technology Co., Ltd. (Shenzhen, China). The fatty acid composition of POSt was identified as lauric acid (0.11 ± 0.01 %), myristic acid (1.35 ± 0.07 %), palmitic acid (54.94 ± 0.75 %), stearic acid (6.95 ± 0.23 %), oleic acid (29.40 ± 0.28 %), linoleic acid (6.85 ± 0.17 %) and arachidic acid (0.40 ± 0.02 %) in our previous research [24]. NaCas (C8654, HLB = 14, pI = 4–5) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. (Shanghai, China). Sodium azide was provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Dipotassium hydrogenphosphate and potassium dihydrogen phosphate, for preparing phosphate buffered saline (PBS), were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Deionized water was utilized in the emulsion preparation, and all other reagents used were of analytical grade.

ce:section.ce:section-title: HIU applications for generating POSt-based emulsions
ce:section.ce:para: The method outlined by Wan et al. [25] was adapted for the preparation of POSt-based emulsions. The aqueous phase was prepared by dissolving 1 wt% NaCas in 10 mM PBS at pH 7.00, with magnetic stirring for 2 h at 60 °C. Additionally, 0.02 wt% sodium azide was added as an antimicrobial agent to the aqueous phase, which was then stored overnight (12 h) at 4 °C to fully hydrate the protein. For emulsions, the 80 wt% aqueous phase was heated to 70 °C in a water bath and poured over the 20 wt% POSt lipid phase, previously melted at the same temperature. Under various ultrasonic powers (100, 150, 200, 300, and 400 W) with 13 mm probe (20-kHz, VCX500 W, Sonics & Materials Inc, Newtown, USA) and using pulse durations of 10 s on-time and 10 s off-time for 5 min, the mixture underwent HIU treatment. To prevent rapid curing of POSt from affecting the initial dispersion of oil droplets, sonication was performed for one minute, followed by the addition of a 25 °C water bath. After recording the post-acoustic temperature, HIU-assisted emulsions were stored at 4 °C overnight for tempering. The studied parameters of all samples are detailed in Table 1 .

The particle size and total size distribution of emulsions were determined using the laser particle size analyzer (Mastersizer 2000, Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK). The refractive indices of dispersing agent and lipid droplets were assumed to be as 1.330 and 1.470, respectively [25]. Measurements were conducted at 10–20 % obscuration by adding emulsions, diluted 1000-fold with deionized water, to obtain volume-weighted mean diameters (D4,3 ) as per Eq. (1).
(1)
D
4 , 3
= ∑
n i
d
i
4
/ ∑
n i
d
i
3
where
n i
is the number of particles with diameter
d i
.
The long-term stability of emulsions stored at 4 °C was assessed by measuring droplet size and size distribution on days 1, 7, 14, 28, 42, and 180.

The ζ-potential of emulsions was assayed using a Zetasizer Nano ZS (MPT-2, Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK). Emulsions were diluted 100-fold with 10 mM PBS (pH 7.00), and loaded into a cuvette for ζ-potential measurement. Samples were allowed to equilibrated at 25 °C for 120 s prior to data collection.

Microscopy (Axio Imager Z2m, Zeiss, Germany) was employed for the microstructure analysis of emulsions treated with different ultrasonic powers. Optical and polarized light images were recorded at 500 × magnification using a digital camera (LSM 700, Zeiss, Germany). Each photomicrograph presented a typical field for the measured samples.

To assess the crystallinity of bulk POSt (control) and POSt-based emulsions, X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) experiments were carried out with a diffractometer (Smartlab, Rigaku, Japan) using CuKα radiation (40 kV, 30 mA). Scans were performed at 4 °C over a 2θ range of 5 to 30 (2.0°/min). Polymorphic data analysis was conducted using MDI Jade 6 software (Materials Data Inc., Livermore, USA).

Melting profiles of bulk POSt and POSt-based emulsions were determined using a differential scanning calorimeter (DSC 3, Mettler Toledo, Switzerland). Each sample weighing 9–10 mg was placed in a flat-bottomed aluminum pan. Samples were initially heated at 5 °C/min from 4 °C to 80 °C, and several successive heating scans were carried out.

The SFC determination was achieved by a low-resolution nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectrometer (mq-one, Bruker, Germany). After long equilibration (1 h) at 65 °C to ensure complete elimination of crystal memory, the control was measured in a glass NMR tube to obtain SFCPOSt . In addition, SFCemulsion of each emulsion was determined using Eq. (2).
(2)
SFC
emulsion
( % )
=
(
SFC
POSt
× Δ H
emulsion
× 100 )
/
Δ H
bulkPOSt
where
Δ H
emulsion
and
Δ H
bulkPOSt
are determined by the DSC software, which represent the total melting enthalpy of POSt-based emulsions and bulk POSt, respectively.

All experiments were conducted in triplicate, and the results are presented as mean ± standard deviation. Statistical analysis of the data was assessed by one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) using SPSS Statistics 26 (IBM, USA) software at a significance level of 5 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Storage stability of POSt-based emulsions treated with HIU
ce:section.ce:para: To depict the condition of oil–water mixtures after emulsification assisted with HIU, appearance images on days 1 and 180 were recorded in Fig. 1 A. The oil droplets of E100 moved to the upper layer due to their lower density, indicating phase separation occurred. As the ultrasonic power increased from 150 W to 400 W, POSt-based emulsions exhibited a uniform and stable appearance. Li et al. [26] noticed that double emulsion failed to form at low ultrasonic power (50 W and 75 W). The power intensity must reach a threshold value for emulsion formation; otherwise, a coarse mixture may be resulted [27]. With time extended to 180 days, E150, E200, E300 and E400 stored at 4 °C showed no visual collapse.

Average particle size is a critical factor for comparing the stability of emulsions [28]. Fig. 1B shows the mean particle size of POSt-based emulsions stored at 4 °C. Increasing ultrasonic power from 150 W to 200 W significantly decreased the D4,3 of emulsions (P < 0.05). However, ultrasonic power above 300 W did not further reduce the D4,3 of emulsions. Previous studies has shown that excess power input may lead to an increase in droplet size for O/W emulsions [29]. There is commonly an optimum power for maximum effect beyond which aggregation may become predominant [27]. Similar aggregation in ultrasound-assisted emulsion was interpreted as over-squeezing of emulsion droplets caused by cavitation bubbles at excessive power [26]. In this study, the results suggest that 200 W could be more efficient in preparing emulsions. Even so, all emulsions (E150, E200, E300, and E400) standing for two weeks showed no significant change in the D4,3 (P < 0.05). After that, it is noteworthy that the average particle size of all emulsions dropped to 0.5 µm at 180 d. Including the coalescence process, the aging of emulsions could make the droplet population more compact, leading to an increase in D4,3 [30]. However, not a gradual increase in the average droplet size was also revealed in emulsions of milk fat by Melsen [31]. They reported that the different phenomenon could be explained with partial coalescence rather than ‘true’ coalescence, yet in an O/W emulsion fat crystals are required. The fat globules coalesced until space-filling clumps or networks would cream out of the O/W emulsion, remaining smaller globules than the original ones, thereby causing the decrease in the average droplet size of the underling emulsion [8,31]. In addition, E400 showed a rapid particle-sized reduction compared to E150, E200, and E300, which could be related to the kinetic of partial coalescence [32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Partially-coalesced kinetic of POSt-based emulsions treated with HIU
ce:section.ce:para: Boode et al. [5] noted that variations in coalescence efficiencies primarily determined changes in the size distribution of remaining droplets. Fig. 2 A-F illustrate the droplet size distribution of POSt-based emulsions on days 1, 7, 14, 28, 42, and 180. E150, E200, and E300 exhibited a bimodal size distribution, covering ranges of 0.1–0.2 µm and 1.0–2.8 µm (Fig. 2A). The ultimate size of an ultrasonic emulsion is determined by the balance between two opposing processes: droplet break-up and droplet re-coalescence [33]. The physical effects of ultrasound induced by acoustic cavitation can boost the disruption of oil droplets. The most important role of the surfactant (NaCas) is to prevent the immediate re-coalescence of newly formed droplets by rapid adsorption to, and stabilization of, the newly formed interface, which promotes the formation of stable O/W emulsions with small-sized droplets. Since the breaking action produced by acoustic cavitation has a short range, there are large-sized droplets remaining away from the emission surface of the probe. The uneven size distribution of E150, E200, and E300 might be related to the limited volume of ultrasonic action. However, only a unimodal peak ranging from 1.0 to 2.8 µm was observed in the size distribution of E400 (Fig. 2A). With a gradual increase in HIU power, the ultrasonic technique could make droplet size distribution curves narrower and decrease the mean diameter of droplets. However, there is generally an optimum power for maximum effect [34]. Beyond 200 W for POSt-based emulsions, droplet re-coalescence may become predominant, leading to an increased particle size of droplets: this is perhaps due to excessive cavitation phenomena [26]. Up to 14 days (Fig. 2A-C), no significant difference was found in droplet size distribution, aligning with the D4,3 results of all emulsions (Fig. 1B). In Fig. 2D, E150, E200, and E300 transitioned to a unimodal distribution, a result of partial coalescence of small globules. Petrut et al. [9] elucidated that systems with partial coalescence exhibit a bimodal distribution corresponding to remaining droplets and emerging clumps (above the range of original droplets), respectively. In this study, another clump distribution peak was not evident, likely due to sampling without premixed operation. Partial coalescence, to some extend, could increase viscosity to enhance emulsion stability, as continuous aqueous phase was entrapped in the clumps of the partially coalesced globules [35]. For E400, a size distribution that remained largely unchanged in Fig. 2D was presumed to be associated with the poorest stability that followed. In comparison to other emulsions at 42 d (Fig. 2E), it was observed that the burst of partial coalescence in E400 was evidenced by a significant increase in the volume of large particle sizes. A few fat clumps rapidly formed solid-like networks and even unexpected cream layers, resulting in the decrease of D4,3 in the remaining emulsions. However, this early partial coalescence might prolong the storage of all emulsions, as indicated by the slowing tendency for D4,3 change in Fig. 1B. With time extended to 180 d, all remaining emulsions in Fig. 2F showed a size distribution with smaller droplets (0.1–1.0 µm) than the original. The results, especially for E400, suggest that most clumps were disposed in space-filling networks, with the exception of tiny-sized globules in remaining emulsions. Unfortunately, phase separation with an oil layer on top would occur once temperature exceeded the melting point of fat networks [8].
Overall, the varying efficiency to partial coalescence would determine the physical stability of POSt-based emulsions, mainly through changes in the droplet size distribution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interface potential of POSt-based emulsions treated with HIU
ce:section.ce:para: Colloidal interaction forces can increase the distance between oil globules, significantly reducing the probability of protruding crystals breaching the interface film [5]. The presence of proteins dispersing droplets can modify the surface charge [36]. Moreover, Taha et al. [37] observed that HIU could form stable emulsions by increasing the amount of adsorbed protein at the lipid droplet interface. The diffusion of NaCas (PI = 4–5) from the continuous phase to POSt-based droplets was observed through potential measurements in Fig. 3 . Within error limits, the ζ-potential values were around −20 mV for all emulsions at pH 7. The ζ-potential reduces interfacial tension by inducing repulsive forces, contributing to the appearance stability of emulsions (Fig. 1A). Ultrasonicated protein alters the tertiary structure and exposes more hydrophobic groups [38]. Hence, the average values of ζ-potential for POSt-based emulsions were higher than that observed for bulk NaCas (−14.6 mV), enhancing the emulsifying properties of protein at the interface [39]. Li et al. [21] described that HIU treatment significantly increased the adsorbed concentration of NaCas, thus enhancing the interfacial properties of NaCas-prepared pre-emulsified soybean oil. Each time the droplets were broken up by ultrasound, more new interfaces for protein adsorption were created, facilitating the formation of smaller droplets. At high ultrasonic power, the oil droplet collision-coalescence time might be shorter than the protein coverage time, leading to anomalous trends towards larger particle size. The similar potential (−20 mV) provided by NaCas without significant difference can alleviate partial coalescence for all emulsified POSt.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Crystal microstructure of POSt-based emulsions treated with HIU
ce:section.ce:para: Previous studies have described that the crystallisation behavior of fat governs factors affecting the rate of partial coalescence [8]. After ultrasonic processing, POSt-based emulsions were fully crystallized by tempering at 4 °C for 24 h. Ordinary light was used to observe the arrangement of the oil globules in Fig. 4 . Meanwhile, the appearance of fat crystals in the oil globules was characterized by polarized light microscopy. According to the four crystallization types (O-, N-, L-, and M−type) of emulsion droplets containing fat crystals summarized by Fredrick et al. [8], the crystals in POSt-based emulsions could be regarded as layered (L-) type that orientated tangentially near the droplet boundary. Boekel et al. [40] suggested that L/M−type globules are more prone to partial coalescence because of the alignment of crystals at the O/W interface. Furthermore, the non-uniform size and ill-trimmed shape of the original crystal caused an increasing sensitivity to partial coalescence in emulsions. Compared to other powers, it was apparent that semi-crystalline globules of E200 displayed more uniform and integral crystal clusters that were distributed tan-gentially near the droplet boundary. The cavitation and mechanical effects of ultrasound were utilized to break down the crystals, forming smaller crystal nuclei and promoting lipid crystallization [41]. However, excessive sonication parameters might lead to strong thermal effects, hindering the formation of crystal nuclei [42], thereby resulting in incomplete clusters. When crystal-defective droplets collided together, crystal protrusions penetrated the interface film and caused the accumulation of oil globules [43]. This supports the findings in Fig. 2 that E200 featured the better storage stability and a slower kinetic of partial coalescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Crystal polymorphism of POSt-based emulsions treated with HIU
ce:section.ce:para: Polymorphism is described as the ability of a substance to be present in more than one type of crystalline lattices [44]. For instance, three arrangements of α, β′, and β are typical polymorphic forms of fats, which affect the melting and crystallization behavior of the system tremendously [45]. For their short spacings, the least stable α-form had a peak at 4.1 Å, the metastable β'-form is between 4.2 Å and 3.8 Å, and the thermodynamically favorable β-form appears at 4.6 Å [46–48].

To determine the crystallographic structure, it is necessary to follow bulk POSt and POSt-based emulsions using XRD at short spacing (Fig. 5 ). The β′-form (five peaks of 4.32, 4.26, 4.20, 3.90, and 3.78 Å) is the dominant species in bulk POSt, along with the α-form as a tiny peak (4.04 Å). Although the signal (three peaks of 4.32, 4.19 and 3.85 Å) of the β'-form diminishes, all POSt-based emulsions display a new peak (4.55 Å) characteristic of the β-form in Fig. 5. Due to the dilution of the emulsified fat in the continuous phase, signal strength and resolution in the emulsified systems are substantially decreased. It is acknowledged that ultrasound could foster the transition of the crystals to a stable polymorphic form in bulk fat [49]. Encouragingly, ultrasonic treatment shows the same effect in crystalline emulsions with low fat concentration,inducing the occurrence of the β-form compared to the initial bulk POSt. As the HIU power increased from 150 W to 400 W, both β- and β′-form of peaks exhibited narrower breadth and higher intensity, indicating that ultrasonication promoted the integrity of fat crystals in POSt-based emulsions (Fig. 5). In the long term, HIU treatment might be regarded as a useful tool for controlling polymorphic crystallization in emulsified fat. However, previous research on sonocrystallization in lard-based emulsions found that there was no obvious effect on crystal transition to the β-form with increasing ultrasound time. One possible explanation is the variation in the effect of different ultrasound parameters on crystallization [23]. Additionally, the type of fat in emulsions might also be a influential factor in ultrasonic crystallization. In the case of using HIU treatment, the results observed were entirely different for interesterified soybean oil [50] and cocoa butter [51], with only the latter inducing the formation of a stable polymorphic form.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal behavior of POSt-based emulsions treated with HIU
ce:section.ce:para: The phase behavior of crystals is important for the physical properties of fat-based emulsions. As shown in Fig. 6 , the non-isothermal melting curves of bulk POSt and POSt-based emulsions were recorded during heating in the range of 4 to 80 °C. Bulk POSt exhibited a broad range with one slight melting peak and two pronounced melting peaks, consistent with the peak temperature of melting (near 10, 40, and 54 °C) for POSt in a bulk state [52]. It is expected that POSt with multiple triacylglycerol compositions may complicate the DSC results, including low- and high-melting transitions [53].

For emulsified POSt, it was found that three exothermic peaks were similar to the bulk POSt, yet single endothermic peak decreased. A widespread tendency was that the stable β-form possessed higher melting temperature, followed by β' and α, corresponding to each respective exothermic peak [45]. Based on the crystal forms (Fig. 5), Peak 1 could be related to the less stable β′-type, and Peak 2 to the stable β-form (Fig. 6). Furthermore, the remarkable occurrence of β-form in Fig. 5 indicated that Peak 2 in POSt-based emulsions, unlike bulk POSt, was not entirely dependent on solid state or melt-mediated transitions derived from metastable forms. The effect of ultrasound on melting profiles in emulsions had often been given poor consideration, owing to there was little fat in these systems. However, little changes in the intensity and shape of melting peaks in POSt-based emulsions (20 %, w/w) were detected in this study (Fig. 6). Ye et al. [50] indicated that no difference in the enthalpy values was measured in the bulk fat (interesterified soybean oil) with and without HIU, perhaps due to the effect of HIU being mainly the generation of smaller crystals rather than the amount of fat crystals. The result was contrary to Jiang et al. [43], who observed sharper melting peaks and stronger melting enthalpy in emulsified lard (80 %, v/v) after HIU, likely caused by the content and type of solid fat. With the inclusion of HIU, the Tp1 and Ton of the POSt in emulsions showed an increasing tendency (P < 0.05), as observed in the melting parameters in Table 2 . This might be due to the formation of a stable β-form during crystallization, as analyzed from XRD results [54].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Crystal formation of POSt-based emulsions treated with HIU
ce:section.ce:para: The temperature environment during emulsion preparation determines the state of POSt, and consequently, the thermal energy released during different ultrasound powers is of interest. The post-acoustic temperatures of emulsions, as shown in Table 3 , reveal the significance of calorimetric measurements, indicating the conversion of acoustic energy into heat [27]. It is noteworthy that E300 and E400 exceeded the melting point of POSt (51.0 °C), leading to their crystallization primarily starting with a 4 °C tempering process at the end of sonication.

Solid fat content (SFC) is a crucial parameter of for describing crystalline fat. In Table 4 , the SFC of E200 was slightly higher than that of other sonicated emulsions stored at 4 °C (24 h). Similar results in anhydrous milk fat were reported by Martini et al. [10], who observed that HIU could generate smaller crystals and induce crystallization at 24, 26, and 28 °C, dependeding on the supercooling. During the sonication process, E200 presented a few crystals at intermediate supercoolings (40.4 °C). The interaction of these crystals with the bubbles produced by the acoustic waves increased the number of smaller crystals, forming more nucleation sites to accelerate the crystallization of fat. However, E150, with high supercooling (35.6 °C), exhibited a decrease in crystallisation content. In this case, Martini et al. [10] suggested that ultrasound only delayed crystallization when high supercooling was used. Gao et al. [23] explained that cavitation bubbles had dissolved before the lipids crystallised in high supercooling. Additionally, the low supercooling (51.1 °C) showed the predictable result, as the higher the temperature, the lower the driving force for crystallization to occur.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Emulsification mechanisms for HIU induced POSt-based emulsions
ce:section.ce:para: To illustrate the effects of HIU treatment on POSt-based emulsions, a speculative mechanism model was depicted in Fig. 7 . Initially, the physical effects of acoustic cavitation disrupted the melted POSt-water interfacial layer, where NaCas rapidly dispersed on the surface of dispersed oil droplets, with hydrophobic groups facing away from the aqueous phase. Excessive ultrasound power instead exacerbated collisions and aggregation, leading to an increased particle size of droplets, consistent with the findings of Li et al. [55]. HIU could alter the emulsifier structure first and impact the stability of the emulsion [56]. When the HIU power increased from 150 W to 400 W, no difference in the ζ-potential result (Fig. 3) evidenced that the interaction forces generated by NaCas had a similar contribution to resisting partial coalescence. The other decisive way in which ultrasound acted on the emulsion was determined by changing the crystal structure in situ, thus directly affecting its stability. Further, POSt-based emulsions were cooled in water bath, and the HIU process was accompanied by the crystallization of the POSt. The impact force generated by the rupture of cavitation bubbles could strongly shatter the crystals to generate more crystal nuclei. It helped to accelerate the secondary nucleation stage and eventually formed homogeneous crystal clusters. However, with the increase in power, acoustic energy was significantly converted into thermal effects, reducing the crystalline subcooling given by external conditions, thereby making subcooling the dominant factor that affected crystallization. Low subcooling (51.1 °C) meant a weak crystallization drive force, producing fewer nuclei and larger tempered clusters. At high supercooling (35.6 °C), the cavitation bubbles dissolved rapidly and also weaken the promotion of fat crystallization by ultrasound [23]. In addition, HIU facilitated the exposure of protein hydrophobic groups and enhanced the repulsive force on the surface of oil droplets [57], which might also improve the stability of POSt-based emulsions.

HIU proves to be a valuable processing technique for customizing lipid crystal properties in bulk fats for industrial applications. This study demonstrated that HIU could induce a polycrystalline transition to a stable β-form in emulsified POSt (20 %, w/w), mirroring the bulk POSt and exhibiting a positive correlation with power levels. Notably, the heat generated during sonication significantly influenced the modification efficiency of HIU, with variations attributed to differences in crystalline supercooling. For intermediate supercooling (40.4 °C) at 200 W, HIU induced fat crystallization with a more pronounced reduction in crystal size and enhancement of cluster integrity, leading to a reduction in the instability of partial coalescence in emulsified POSt. The HIU-treated NaCas acted as a reinforced emulsifier, contributing to the stability of emulsified POSt, as indicated by a −20 mV zeta potential (P < 0.05). In summary, HIU treatment influenced the stability of POSt-based emulsions through two primary mechanisms: structural modification of fat crystals or emulsifiers. The former directly determined the destabilization kinetics of the emulsion, as demonstrated in this study. It is evident that HIU has the potential to enhance the physical properties of crystalline emulsions. However, achieving optimal application of HIU requires careful control of extrinsic factors such as power intensity and crystallization supercooling.

Ying Song: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Jiawei Xiao: Visualization, Software, Methodology, Investigation. Lin Li: Resources. Liting Wan: Methodology, Investigation. Bing Li: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Xia Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, SCUT (No. 2022ZYGXZR058), the National Key R&D Program of China (No. 2022YFD2101404), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. U1905202& 32172225), Guangdong Special Support Program (2021TQ06N126), Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (No.2020A1515011304).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Yu, S. Li, S. Sun, H. Yan, H. Zou, Modification of emulsifying properties of mussel myofibrillar proteins by high-intensity ultrasonication treatment and the stability of O/W emulsion, Colloids Surf. Physicochem. Eng. Aspects 641 (2022) 128511.
